segments.title: Why Britain preferred an independent Hawaii
segments.summary: Britain valued Hawaii as an open, neutral stopping place for warships and merchants but saw little advantage in acquiring and defending the islands.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke opens by challenging the idea that Britain wanted to annex Hawaii. For the United States, the islands mattered to trans-Pacific trade; for Britain, whose main imperial routes ran through the Atlantic, Mediterranean, and Indian Ocean, Hawaii was chiefly a useful neutral port where ships could obtain supplies. Hawaiian rulers tried to preserve independence by balancing British and American interests, but the kingdom lacked the power and preparedness to dictate how stronger states behaved. He describes an advanced and organized state whose reform capacity was fragile, noting that ministers who travelled abroad to learn how other powers worked repeatedly died before they could guide Hawaii through the later nineteenth century. The Paulet Affair grew from threats to that balance rather than a British plan of conquest.

evidence.note: The introduction contrasts American strategic interest with Britain's preference for an open neutral port and explains Hawaii's balancing strategy.

segments.title: Religious and commercial disputes became a diplomatic crisis
segments.summary: Conflicts involving British consul Richard Charlton, Protestant missionaries, Catholic worship, merchants, and Hawaiian courts accumulated over nearly two decades.
segments.body: Richard Charlton combined his unpaid consular role with private commercial interests, making disputes over land, marriages, court cases, and religious policy matters of state as well as business. Dr. Clarke emphasizes the influence of the conservative missionary Hiram Bingham and the exclusion faced by Catholics and other Christians outside his preferred tradition. One early dispute involved Captain William Buckle, whom missionary William Richards accused of trafficking a woman after Buckle made what Clarke interprets as a dowry payment and married outside the missionaries' church. The attempt to pursue a libel case against Richards then collided with royal support for the missionary, illustrating how incompatible religious, legal, and commercial assumptions accumulated into national grievances. Royal Navy visits had already supported religious balance, including HMS Sulphur's delivery of Catholic priests in 1837. By 1842 Charlton believed Hawaiian proceedings were systematically prejudiced against him and left to seek British support.

evidence.note: The transcript traces Charlton's appointment, missionary influence, religious conflicts, court disputes, and his departure to present his case.

segments.title: Merchant rivalry trapped the Royal Navy in a national dispute
segments.summary: Charlton's account turned his conflict with Hawaiian minister Gerrit Judd into an apparent insult to the British Crown that Captain Lord George Paulet could not ignore.
segments.body: Charlton encountered Captain Lord George Paulet of HMS Carysfort and described hostile judgments made while he was away. Because Charlton was both a merchant and the Crown's consul, attacks on his position appeared to Paulet as attacks on the British government. Gerrit P. Judd was likewise both a participant in commercial rivalry and a senior Hawaiian minister. Their overlapping private and public roles transformed a contest between merchants into a confrontation between states, leaving a naval officer with force as his most obvious means of protecting British standing.

evidence.note: Paulet receives Charlton's allegations, while the dual official and commercial roles of Charlton and Judd are identified as the central danger.

segments.title: Rejecting the acting consul removed the diplomatic safety valve
segments.summary: Hawaii's refusal to recognize Charlton's deputy left HMS Carysfort communicating directly with the government and deprived both sides of an experienced intermediary.
segments.body: When Paulet arrived, Kamehameha III was away from Honolulu and Charlton's appointed replacement, Alexander Simpson, was not recognized as acting consul. That decision may have helped Charlton's opponents in court, but it also removed the official who could have explained local delays and mediated British complaints. Paulet was not a Pacific specialist and had less diplomatic flexibility than a consul. Dr. Clarke presents this as a procedural victory that exposed the Hawaiian government to a much harder naval response.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the king's absence, normal expectations of naval diplomacy, and the consequences of refusing Simpson's status.

segments.title: A refused private interview hardened Paulet's position
segments.summary: Paulet requested a direct meeting with Kamehameha III, but the king referred him to Judd—the official at the center of the British complaints.
segments.body: Paulet's initial correspondence asked when the king could return and then requested a private interview with a competent interpreter. Kamehameha III declined and offered Judd as his confidential agent. Since Paulet regarded Judd as the prime mover behind the alleged injuries to British subjects, the reply made compromise harder rather than easier. Paulet refused to deal with Judd and converted his complaints into demands backed by a deadline and the threat of coercive action.

evidence.note: The letters requesting and refusing a private interview are read and Paulet's objection to negotiating through Judd is explained.

segments.title: Paulet's demands focused on consular standing and legal treatment
segments.summary: The ultimatum sought restoration of Charlton's property, recognition of Simpson, safeguards for British prisoners, rehearings, mixed juries, and direct consular access.
segments.body: Paulet did not demand annexation. His six points addressed the disputed attachment of Charlton's property, the rejection of the acting consul, imprisonment of British subjects for minor or religious offenses, a promised retrial, jury composition, and direct communication between the king and British representative. Some provisions look intrusive by modern standards, especially mixed juries, but Dr. Clarke places them within existing nineteenth-century agreements intended to manage cases between foreigners and local residents. USS Boston remained in harbor, yet its captain would not start a war over commercial actors who had allowed their rivalry to become an international crisis.

evidence.note: The six demands are read and interpreted, followed by discussion of their legal context and the American ship's non-intervention.

segments.title: Failed negotiations led to a provisional cession
segments.summary: After several meetings failed to reconcile legal process with British demands, Kamehameha III provisionally ceded the islands pending a decision by Queen Victoria's government.
segments.body: The Hawaiian government recognized Simpson and reopened communication, but disagreement remained over whether the king could or should reverse court decisions. On 25 February 1843, Kamehameha III signed a conditional cession to Paulet. The document explicitly reserved the outcome for negotiations already under way and for the British government's judgment after receiving full information. The act was therefore an appeal upward through imperial authority as well as a surrender to local pressure.

evidence.note: Daily meetings, the dispute over court authority, and the king's conditional deed of cession are described and read.

segments.title: Paulet preserved Hawaiian administration under temporary control
segments.summary: Paulet's proclamation kept the king, chiefs, laws, revenue officers, and archives in place while a commission controlled foreign relations and froze land transfers.
segments.body: The British flag was raised, but Paulet's commission did not replace the whole Hawaiian state. Native government continued for the Hawaiian population, existing laws remained the basis of justice, revenue officials and archives stayed in local hands, and bona fide obligations remained valid. The commission concentrated on relations among foreign residents and prohibited new land sales or transfers until London decided the issue. This limited arrangement reflected the original dispute over consular property and the provisional nature of the cession.

evidence.note: Paulet's proclamation sets out the commission, continued native administration, existing laws, revenue arrangements, and land-transfer freeze.

segments.title: Hawaiian and British envoys carried rival accounts abroad
segments.summary: Simpson sailed for Britain while James F. B. Marshall sought American and Hawaiian diplomatic support, but London had already chosen to recognize Hawaiian independence.
segments.body: Paulet sent Simpson to explain events since Charlton's departure, while Judd dispatched the merchant James F. B. Marshall to present Hawaii's case in Boston and London. Marshall cultivated newspapers, American officials, and Hawaiian envoys already negotiating in Europe. Their journeys did not determine Britain's basic policy: Foreign Secretary Lord Aberdeen had stated on 1 April that Britain intended to recognize the independence of the Sandwich Islands under their existing sovereign. The remaining problem was finding a settlement that protected British interests without humiliating either government.

evidence.note: The routes and advocacy of Simpson and Marshall are traced against Aberdeen's earlier recognition of Hawaiian independence.

segments.title: The U.S. Pacific Squadron watched without forcing a confrontation
segments.summary: American warships monitored the crisis, but Commodore Thomas ap Catesby Jones recognized that neither the dispute nor the naval balance justified war with Britain.
segments.body: USS Constellation and then USS United States arrived while Paulet's provisional government remained in place. Commodore Thomas ap Catesby Jones consulted American interests and observed events but did not attempt to expel the British. The local forces were reasonably balanced, and Washington could see that American and British merchants had helped create the crisis. Clarke also points to weak congressional support for U.S. naval infrastructure as another reason not to risk a new war with Britain over a merchant-driven dispute. He uses Jones's restraint to reject stories in which an American frigate simply compelled the Royal Navy to back down.

evidence.note: The arrival of American ships, Jones's restraint, the local balance, and the need for a negotiated reversal are discussed.

segments.title: Admiral Thomas restored Kamehameha III's sovereignty
segments.summary: Rear Admiral Richard Thomas reached Honolulu, met the king, secured an acceptable settlement, and rejected the provisional cession on 31 July 1843.
segments.body: As commander-in-chief on the Pacific Station, Rear Admiral Richard Dartmouth Thomas possessed wider diplomatic authority than Paulet. He arrived on 26 July, requested a personal interview with Kamehameha III, and established that the grievances could be resolved without ending Hawaiian independence. Five days later he formally declined the cession, recognized Kamehameha III as the legitimate sovereign, and restored administration of justice to Hawaiian hands. The declaration paired that restoration with expectations that genuine injuries to British subjects would receive redress.

evidence.note: Thomas's arrival, interview, assessment, and declaration restoring Kamehameha III are recounted and read.

segments.title: A station commander could combine naval and diplomatic power
segments.summary: Thomas had the authority to offer compromise while retaining a credible warning that repeated injustice could bring a much larger naval response.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts the relatively binary tools available to a frigate captain with the authority of a regional commander-in-chief. Thomas could negotiate new proposals, restore sovereignty, and speak for British policy while also commanding the force needed to compel redress if diplomacy failed. His declaration promised respect for independent states, including weak ones, but insisted that governments protect foreign residents and restrain ministers who turned private quarrels into national affronts. The result was an early example of naval force supporting, rather than replacing, a negotiated settlement.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Thomas's greater authority, the settlement's reciprocal obligations, and the force behind its diplomatic language.

segments.title: How to read nineteenth-century diplomatic boilerplate
segments.summary: Thomas's long declaration shows how a few operative promises and warnings could be embedded within conventional language about civilization, religion, commerce, and friendship.
segments.body: The restoration declaration closes with extended claims about Captain Cook, George Vancouver, American missionaries, religion, civilization, justice, and commerce. Dr. Clarke cautions that this language was conventional diplomatic boilerplate rather than a sequence of separate policy decisions. The historian's task is to isolate the few actionable sentences: Britain restored Hawaiian sovereignty, expected redress for genuine injuries, and reserved a commander's right to act if justice were later denied. Reading the document this way helps distinguish period rhetoric from the commitments that changed what governments and naval officers would actually do. It also explains why an apparently conciliatory declaration could still carry a credible coercive warning.

evidence.note: Clarke identifies conventional nineteenth-century phraseology and separates the declaration's few actionable provisions from its extended diplomatic rhetoric.

segments.title: Thomas brought diplomatic experience and HMS Dublin's firepower
segments.summary: Thomas's long naval career, experience around South America and Tahiti, and powerful razeed flagship gave his conciliatory diplomacy substantial credibility.
segments.body: Thomas had served from the French Revolutionary Wars onward, commanded numerous ships, supervised vessels in ordinary, and handled difficult relations in the Pacific. His work with Americans earned respect beyond the Royal Navy, while experience of the French occupation of Tahiti prepared him for sovereignty disputes. HMS Dublin, a razeed third rate carrying heavy 32-pounder armament, was arguably the strongest ship present at ordinary fighting ranges. Thomas therefore arrived with both the professional judgment and material leverage needed to make compromise persuasive.

evidence.note: Thomas's service, diplomatic reputation, HMS Dublin's classification, armament, and comparison with American ships are surveyed.

segments.title: Judd's early miscalculation narrowed Paulet's options
segments.summary: Trying to make Judd the intermediary when he was the subject of complaint encouraged a direct, forceful officer to treat the crisis as a problem for coercion.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that a prompt personal meeting between Kamehameha III and Paulet might have prevented the affair from escalating. Directing Paulet to negotiate through Judd instead put the accused minister between the captain and the king. Paulet probably went too far, but his temperament and restricted authority favored a hard, procedural response. His later promotion, Crimean War service, and respected Royal Navy career complicate any picture of him as a rogue officer acting wholly outside professional expectations. Thomas succeeded partly because experience, rank, and broader powers allowed him to think laterally and reopen the encounter on different terms.

evidence.note: The escalation is traced to the proposed use of Judd as intermediary and contrasted with the experience and flexibility Thomas brought.

segments.title: The restoration agreement rewrote consular relations
segments.summary: The settlement guaranteed equal treatment, direct naval access to the king, consular protections, fairer trials, and referral of unresolved claims to London.
segments.body: Kamehameha III pledged most-favored-nation treatment to British subjects and agreed to receive visiting British captains when the purpose of an interview was properly stated. The agreement protected interpreters from acting as political filters, restored disputed consular property, limited the use of fetters, and provided British participation in juries and appeals involving British residents. It also recognized acting consuls while allowing Hawaii to object for stated cause and sent unresolved disputes to the British government rather than leaving merchants or local officers to escalate them. The central lesson was institutional: private commercial quarrels had to be separated from ministerial and consular office.

evidence.note: The ten-article agreement is read and interpreted as a framework for access, property, trials, consular recognition, appeals, and independent sovereignty.

segments.title: Britain rejected annexation and moved toward professional diplomats
segments.summary: British policy consistently favored Hawaiian independence, while the affair exposed the danger of giving private merchants consular authority.
segments.body: Neither the demands nor the settlement proposed permanent British rule, and Aberdeen had already committed the government to recognizing the existing Hawaiian sovereign. American and British naval officers socialized throughout the apparent confrontation, helping defuse any risk of a battle between their ships. Paulet accepted the limits he had set and yielded authority as soon as Thomas arrived. In the longer term, Britain increasingly moved away from relying on merchants who combined personal business with diplomatic office.

evidence.note: The discussion reiterates Aberdeen's policy, naval social diplomacy, Paulet's conduct, and the shift toward professional diplomatic representation.

segments.title: Restoration Day and Thomas Square commemorated the settlement
segments.summary: Kamehameha III proclaimed amnesty and celebration, while the flag ceremony and Thomas Square preserved the memory of Admiral Thomas's restoration of Hawaiian rule.
segments.body: The king's proclamation protected subjects who had served during the provisional government, released prisoners, and suspended ordinary business for ten days of celebration. At the restoration ceremony the British flag came down, the Hawaiian flag rose, and Royal Navy ships and shore guns fired salutes. Honolulu's Thomas Square was named for the admiral and marks the place associated with the restoration. Dr. Clarke notes its continuing status as one of the locations where the Hawaiian flag may fly without the United States flag.

evidence.note: The king's post-restoration proclamation, celebrations, flag ceremony, naval salutes, and Thomas Square are described.

segments.title: The French occupation of 1849 followed a harsher pattern
segments.summary: Six years later French forces used similar religious and commercial grievances but landed Marines, seized a fort, destroyed guns and property, and took the king's yacht.
segments.body: French complaints again centered on the treatment of Catholics and restrictions on trade, but the 1849 response was more openly destructive than Paulet's provisional administration. About 140 Marines occupied an almost empty fort, spiked or destroyed its coastal guns, caused extensive damage, and carried off the king's yacht. Judd sought help in the United States, Britain, and France, including from British naval figures connected to the earlier affair. Paris ultimately accepted its officers' justification and refused reparations, showing the limits of Hawaii's ability to obtain redress even after the lessons of 1843.

evidence.note: The causes, landing force, seizure and damage, Judd's mission, and French refusal of reparations are recounted.

segments.title: Repeated foreign pressure foreshadowed American annexation
segments.summary: The Paulet Affair and French occupation illustrated the structural weakness that eventually left Hawaii vulnerable to American takeover under related pretexts.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke closes the historical argument by connecting the crises of 1843 and 1849 to Hawaii's eventual absorption by the United States. Britain and France conspicuously avoided permanent annexation, but repeated interventions demonstrated how foreign residents and officials could turn local disputes into coercive action. Hawaii's independence depended on balancing powers whose interests and willingness to intervene were unequal. The case therefore illuminates both naval diplomacy and the survival problem facing a strategically located small state.

evidence.note: The conclusion links repeated foreign intervention and similar pretexts to eventual American control while noting British and French reluctance to annex Hawaii.
